topic_name: The Coordinate Plane and Plotting Points
hook: You already know how to describe locations in everyday life — like saying a coffee shop is '3 blocks east and 2 blocks north' of the library. But what if you needed to describe the exact position of any point in a flat, infinite space, and share that description with someone anywhere in the world so they could find the same spot? How would you create a system that works for every possible location, including places that are 'behind' or 'below' your starting point?
concept: The coordinate plane is a flat grid created by two perpendicular number lines that cross at a shared zero point. Any location on this plane can be described exactly using an ordered pair $(x, y)$, where $x$ tells you how far to move horizontally from the center and $y$ tells you how far to move vertically. The pair is called 'ordered' because the order matters: $(3, 2)$ and $(2, 3)$ are different points.
anatomy: The coordinate plane has five key parts. The horizontal number line is called the x-axis, and the vertical number line is called the y-axis. They intersect at the origin, written as $(0, 0)$. Every point is named by an ordered pair $(x, y)$: the first number is the x-coordinate (also called the abscissa), which measures horizontal distance and direction, and the second number is the y-coordinate (also called the ordinate), which measures vertical distance and direction. The two axes divide the plane into four regions called quadrants, numbered I through IV counterclockwise starting from the upper right.

worked_example.description_latex: We start by identifying what each number in the ordered pair tells us. The x-coordinate is $4$, which means we will move 4 units to the right of the origin, and the y-coordinate is $-3$, which means we will then move 3 units downward. Always handle the x-coordinate first, then the y-coordinate — this is the universal convention.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Start at the origin } (0,0) \text{ and move } 4 \text{ units right along the x-axis}
worked_example.description_latex: Because the x-coordinate is positive ($+4$), we move to the right. Think of the x-axis as a horizontal street: positive values go right (east), and negative values go left (west). We land temporarily at the position $x = 4$ on the x-axis.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{From } x = 4 \text{ on the x-axis, move } 3 \text{ units down}
worked_example.description_latex: Because the y-coordinate is negative ($-3$), we move downward from the x-axis. The y-axis acts like a vertical elevator: positive values go up (north) and negative values go down (south). Moving 3 units down from $x = 4$ on the x-axis places us in Quadrant IV.

We mark and label the point. Since the x-coordinate is positive and the y-coordinate is negative, the point falls in Quadrant IV — the lower-right region of the coordinate plane. Checking the quadrant is a great way to verify you moved in the correct directions.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Plot the point } Q = (-2, 5) \text{ and identify its quadrant}
partial_example.description_latex: We are given the ordered pair $(-2, 5)$. The first step is always to read the signs: the x-coordinate is $-2$ (negative) and the y-coordinate is $5$ (positive). A negative x-coordinate means we move left from the origin, and a positive y-coordinate means we move up.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Start at } (0,0) \text{ and move } 2 \text{ units left, then } 5 \text{ units up}
partial_example.description_latex: We move left 2 units along the x-axis because $x = -2$, then move up 5 units because $y = 5$. This two-step movement always lands us on the correct point — the horizontal move first, then the vertical.

Now it's your turn: based on the moves above, write the coordinates of $Q$, mark the point on the coordinate plane, and state which quadrant it falls in. Remember: Quadrant II has negative x and positive y — does that match what you see here?

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Identify the coordinates of a point located at the origin.}
\text{Plot and label the following points: } A = (3, 4),\ B = (-1, 2),\ C = (-3, -5).\ \text{Which quadrant does each point lie in?}
practice_problems.answer_latex: A \text{ is in Quadrant I},\ B \text{ is in Quadrant II},\ C \text{ is in Quadrant III}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A point has coordinates } (-6, 0). \text{ On which axis does it lie, and what is its distance from the origin?}
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{It lies on the x-axis, at a distance of } 6 \text{ units from the origin.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{The vertices of a rectangle are at } (1, 2),\ (5, 2),\ (5, -1),\ \text{and } (1, -1). \text{ Find the length and width of the rectangle.}

\text{A city map is set up as a coordinate plane. The library is at } (2, 3) \text{ and the park is at } (-4, 3). \text{ If each unit represents 1 block, how many blocks apart are the library and the park, and in which direction would you walk from the library to reach the park?}

common_mistakes: Students often reverse the x- and y-coordinates, plotting $(3, 5)$ as if it were $(5, 3)$. Remember: the ordered pair is always $(x, y)$ — x comes first because we move horizontally before vertically, just as we read left-to-right.

When one coordinate is negative, students sometimes move in the wrong direction — for example, treating $x = -4$ as a move to the right instead of the left. Fix this by always pausing to read the sign before moving: negative x means left, negative y means down, every single time.

Students frequently confuse points that lie on an axis (like $(0, -3)$ or $(5, 0)$) with points in a quadrant. A point on an axis does not belong to any quadrant — quadrants are the four interior regions, and the axes form the boundaries between them.
untested_variants: Reflecting a point across one or both axes (e.g., finding the reflection of $(-3, 4)$ across the x-axis) and comparing coordinate signs.
Identifying or plotting points where both coordinates are fractions or decimals, such as $(1.5, -2.75)$.
Using the coordinate plane in all four quadrants to calculate the distance between two points that are NOT on the same horizontal or vertical line (requiring the distance formula).
Reading coordinates off a graph where the axes are scaled by values other than 1 (e.g., each grid square represents 2 units or 10 units).
